title: Mappings ŌĆö `:map`, `:noremap`, and Friends
subtitle: How to bind a keystroke to a command in every mode, and what the modifiers actually do.
summary: Vim has one mapping system with a per-mode entry point. `:nnoremap` is the workhorse ŌĆö non-recursive *normal mode* map. `<silent>`, `<expr>`, and `<buffer>` are the three modifiers that cover 90% of real mappings.

blocks.text: A *mapping* binds a keystroke (or short sequence) to a command. It's how you build a personal layer on top of Vim's grammar ŌĆö leader shortcuts, plugin bindings, language-specific helpers. The mapping system has more knobs than most people realize, and most of the bugs in handwritten vimrcs come from picking the wrong knob.
Mappings are scoped to a mode. Picking the right command (instead of bare `:map`) is the single most important habit.

rows: Bindings that act on the buffer from rest. The default for almost every leader mapping.
Live-while-typing shortcuts ŌĆö autopairs, abbreviation-like helpers, completion triggers.
Rarely what you want; the Select half eats keystrokes meant as text. Prefer `:xmap`.
Visual-mode bindings (e.g. {key:>} to indent and re-select).
Almost never. *Select mode* behaves like a text-replacing selection (think other editors).
Custom motions and text objects. After {key:d}/{key:c}/{key:y}, your map fires.
Bindings inside `:terminal` buffers (Neovim and newer Vim).
The bare form. Almost always too broad.
The other broad form. Sometimes useful for {key:Ctrl}-chords you want everywhere you type.

blocks.text: `map` vs `noremap` ŌĆö the one you almost always want

Every mode has a `nore` variant: `:nnoremap`, `:inoremap`, `:vnoremap`, etc. The `nore` prefix means *non-recursive*: the right-hand side is interpreted using Vim's built-in commands, not other mappings.

Without `nore`, your mapping can be re-mapped by another mapping. `:nmap j gj` then `:nmap gj jjj` will loop. With `:nnoremap j gj`, the `gj` on the right is the *built-in* {key:g}{key:j}, and the mapping is safe. *Use `nore` everywhere unless you have a specific reason not to.* "Specific reason" is almost always: you want to invoke another plugin's mapping deliberately.

rows: Don't echo the right-hand side on the command line as the mapping runs.
Almost always for mappings that call commands. Without it, the `:somecmd`{key:Enter} flicker is visible every keystroke.
Evaluate the right-hand side as a Vimscript expression; the *result* is what gets typed.
Conditional mappings ŌĆö "if the popup menu is visible, do X; otherwise do Y." The classic `<expr>` use-case is wiring {key:Tab} for completion.
Mapping applies only in the current buffer. Stored on the buffer, gone when the buffer is wiped.
Filetype-specific mappings set from `ftplugin/`. Keeps your Python bindings out of your Markdown buffers.

blocks.text: Modifiers go *before* the keystroke, in any order:

blocks.title: The mapping-mode matrix
blocks.text:
Vim's documentation summarizes the system with a 7├Ś8 matrix. Each row is a mode; each column is a `:map` command that fires in that mode. The diagonal you want is the *minimum-overlap* set:

```
              Cmd        Normal  Visual  Select  Op-pending  Insert  Cmdline  Term-job
              ---        ------  ------  ------  ----------  ------  -------  --------
  :map         (-)         ŌĆó       ŌĆó       ŌĆó         ŌĆó
  :nmap        n           ŌĆó
  :vmap        v                   ŌĆó       ŌĆó
  :xmap        x                   ŌĆó
  :smap        s                           ŌĆó
  :omap        o                                     ŌĆó
  :map!        (-)                                              ŌĆó        ŌĆó
  :imap        i                                              ŌĆó
  :cmap        c                                                        ŌĆó
  :tmap        t                                                                  ŌĆó
```

Read it as: *which `:map` command fires in which mode*. Bare `:map` lights up four modes ŌĆö that's why it's almost never the right answer. `:vmap` accidentally hits *select mode* too, which is why `:xmap` (Visual-only) is what most people actually want. `:omap` is the only way to define a custom text object or operator-aware motion; it pairs with `:onoremap` and is the entire reason operator-pending (see [*Operator-Pending Mode*](#text-objects.op-pending)) is exposed as its own mode in the first place.

The `nore` variants (`:nnoremap` etc.) occupy the same columns but disable recursive expansion of the right-hand side. Run `:help map-modes` for the canonical version.

blocks.title: Useful read-only commands
blocks.text:
Same per-mode prefix, but without arguments lists every mapping in that mode:

  ŌĆó `:nmap` ŌĆö list every *normal mode* mapping.
  ŌĆó `:nmap <leader>` ŌĆö list every mapping that starts with your leader.
  ŌĆó `:verbose nmap <leader>w` ŌĆö show the binding *and* the file/line where it was last set. Indispensable for figuring out which plugin stole your shortcut.
  ŌĆó `:map <buffer>` ŌĆö list buffer-local mappings only.

In Neovim, the Lua spelling is:

```
vim.keymap.set('n', '<leader>w', ':write<CR>', { silent = true })
```

ŌĆö same matrix, same modifiers (`silent`, `expr`, `buffer`, plus `desc` for which-key style helpers).
If a mapping "doesn't work," the first debug step is `:verbose nmap lhs`. It will name the file that defined whatever currently owns that keystroke ŌĆö usually a plugin you forgot you installed.
